headline: GRAINS FIND BOTTOM, LIVESTOCK SLIPS ON USDA SUPPLY VIEW
subheadline: Corn leads grain bounce while cattle complex falls on milk production outlook.
lead: Corn closed $4.13, up half a cent and looking like it finally found the floor the funds have been hunting for three weeks. December corn at $4.41 tells the story better: new-crop's firming while old-crop stays defensive, the calendar spread working exactly like it should when planting's done and the weather hasn't turned. The grain complex answered this week's question with a definitive yes, breaking the five-day slide on fund rotation and fresh energy in the ethanol story.
the_takeaway: Corn found its bottom, cattle didn't.
teaser: Grain complex breaks five-day slide as corn finds floor at $4.13.

one_number.unit: barrels per day of Persian Gulf oil moved with U.S. military help
one_number.context: Energy Secretary Chris Wright put a number to why crude's at $84 instead of $150. The U.S. military is actively moving 7 million barrels daily out of the Persian Gulf, keeping supply lines open as Iran-Hormuz tensions continue to ease. That's roughly 7% of global production getting military escort, explaining why the geopolitical premium deflated faster than the headlines suggested.

yesterdays_call.summary: Funds getting defensive ahead of WASDE, Brazil supply weighing on outlook.
yesterdays_call.note: The defensive positioning paid off as grains found their floor right where fund liquidation suggested.

sections.title: CORN LEADS GRAINS HIGHER
sections.body: Corn closed $4.13, up half a cent and finally acting like it wants to stay above $4.10. December corn gained a nickel to $4.41, the new-crop spread telling you the market's done going down and ready to price the weather again. **Fund rotation out of beans into corn** drove the action, managed money adding 8,000 contracts while soybeans stayed flat at $11.13. The 45Z GREET model update from Energy gave ethanol bulls something to work with, biofuel groups calling it a 'major win' for the industry. Rootless corn syndrome confirmed in Indiana adds a production risk premium the market wasn't pricing yesterday.
sections.catalyst: DOE updates 45Z GREET model, biofuel groups call it 'major win'
sections.bottom_line: Corn found the floor, funds rotating in, ethanol story getting legs.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: defensive positioning ended, fund flow reversed into buying.

sections.title: CATTLE COMPLEX FALLS ON USDA OUTLOOK
sections.body: Live cattle dropped $1.50 to $240.95, feeders fell $2.00 to $357.05, both breaking yesterday's bounce on USDA's latest supply and demand report. **Increased milk production per cow and larger cow inventories** are the department's base case for 2026-27, putting pressure on the entire protein complex. The direct business stayed quiet, no cash trade to support the futures, and high beef prices are shifting consumer habits toward ground beef and away from premium cuts. JBS closing the Souderton and Memphis plants removes capacity but the market's pricing oversupply, not processing constraints.
sections.catalyst: USDA projects increased milk supplies, declining cheese prices to overhang market
sections.bottom_line: USDA supply outlook weighing heavier than processing plant closures.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: yesterday's bounce broken by USDA supply projections.

sections.title: OATS FALL ON OVERNIGHT SELLING
sections.body: Oats fell 9.5 cents to $3.08, a 3% drop that caught the market off-guard in overnight trading. **No specific catalyst drove the selling**, looks like fund liquidation in a thin market where small position changes move prices hard. The grain's been trading in a narrow range for weeks, sitting 28% off its 52-week high, and somebody decided Friday morning was the day to get out. Volume was light, the kind of move that happens when liquidity dries up and one fund hits the exit.
sections.catalyst: Fund liquidation in thin overnight market, no specific news catalyst
sections.bottom_line: Thin market, big fund, one-way trade in overnight session.

outside_the_pit.title: Senators push USDA to expand screwworm response measures
outside_the_pit.body: Letter from senators recommends measures USDA should explore to strengthen response to the growing U.S. outbreak. The political pressure is building as the outbreak spreads, suggesting federal resources will expand beyond current containment efforts.

outside_the_pit.title: Tar spot confirmed in Eastern Corn Belt for first time in 2026
outside_the_pit.body: Extension pathologist found the disease in a Randolph County, Indiana test plot in V6 corn planted early April. Disease pressure building earlier than normal could become a yield drag story if conditions stay favorable for spread.

outside_the_pit.title: Europe wary of long-term U.S. LNG commitments despite supply crisis
outside_the_pit.body: European buyers aren't signing long-term deals with U.S. LNG exporters even as they phase out Russian imports and face Middle East supply disruptions. The reluctance suggests Europe expects the current crisis to be temporary.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: New-crop gained a nickel on old-crop today, the calendar spread doing exactly what it should when planting's done and the market's ready to price weather instead of acreage. **Carry's working** means the market believes in the crop, not the headline price.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt corn basis firming on ethanol restart
basis.body: **Corn basis is talking** in the eastern Belt as ethanol plants come back online after maintenance season. Producers with old-crop storage east of the Mississippi have a window the futures board alone isn't pricing. Western Belt staying soft, seasonal pattern holding as rail logistics normalize post-planting.

weekly_thread.question: Will the grain complex break out of its five-day slide this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: Yes. Corn led the complex higher, funds rotated back into grains, and the five-day slide broke on genuine buying, not just short-covering. The week's question got its answer.

the_more_you_know.title: Why the 45Z GREET model matters more than the name suggests
the_more_you_know.body: Today's DOE update to the 45Z Carbon Intensity-GREET model earned biofuel groups' 'major win' label because it determines which ethanol qualifies for the 45Z clean fuel tax credit. **The model calculates lifecycle carbon intensity scores** that determine credit eligibility - lower scores mean higher credits, up to $1 per gallon. Ethanol plants that invested in carbon capture, renewable electricity, or biogas from manure get rewarded with better scores. The model update reportedly makes those investments more valuable, explaining why corn futures found their floor today even with no weather premium and funds that were selling yesterday.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress; corn above 95% planted removes weather premium completely.
Weekly export sales; corn above 500K MT confirms demand holding under $4.15.
Direct cattle trade; cash under $240 confirms the USDA supply outlook is pricing.

daily_quote.text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.

source_summary: CBOT/CME settlements, USDA reports, trade publications, energy sector analysis
